text: Nothing fancy here. This is a forty-eight-year-old female with symptomatic cholelithiasis.
The surgeon retracts tissue using four robotic arms: Arm one equipped with a ProGrasp, Arm two with a Maryland dissector, Arm three holding the camera, and Arm four utilizing hook cautery.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green dye to enhance visualization while dissecting the critical view of safety in the gallbladder area, ensuring clear identification of anatomical structures.
Some of the cases with acute cholecystitis, unfortunately the cystic duct doesn't usually light up, but sometimes it does. But all cases, I pretty much use it.
An aberrant artery is visible running on the lateral side, just to the right of the surgeon's hook cautery, extending up to the cystic plate.
The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety, visualizing the posterior wall of the right hepatic lobe through the opening.

And then I usually use round-tip scissors. It actually saves money than the monopolar scissors. So I usually use round-tip scissors. Saves about, I think, three hundred dollars for the case, something along those lines.
The surgeon peels the gallbladder off the cystic plate, performing a straightforward dissection as part of the cholecystectomy procedure.
